episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Cariboo Voices & Local News
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Cariboo Voices & Local News.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Quesnel Cariboo Observer] B.C. unemployment rate spikes 0.6 percentage points to 6.7 per cent in March and [Williams Lake Tribune] B.C. government easing carbon tax for pulp mills as industry grapples with future, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What constitutes community resilience infrastructure in rural Cariboo communities, and who actually maintains it? Topics include: Fire response infrastructure and volunteer emergency services, Air quality monitoring and vulnerable population access to health information, Cultural institutions as psychological resilience resources, Community foundation endowment models for sustaining local infrastructure, Indigenous Guardian programs as models for sustained locally-rooted capacity.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Quesnel Community Foundation

deep_dive.title: Cariboo Connections - Cariboo Voices & Local News

articles.title: [100 Mile Free Press] South Cariboo firefighters contain blaze on Abel Lake Road
[Williams Lake Tribune] Elevated pollution prompts air quality warning in Williams Lake
[Williams Lake Tribune] Williams Lake Film Club presents ‘H is for Hawk’

discussion.central_question: What constitutes community resilience infrastructure in rural Cariboo communities, and who actually maintains it?
discussion.riley_position: Community resilience depends on distributed informal infrastructure—relationships, institutions, and local knowledge networks—that work behind the scenes and are chronically underrecognized by formal funding systems.
riley_key_evidence: Abel Lake Road fire containment resulted from years of training and local knowledge in South Cariboo fire coverage
Williams Lake Film Club screening of 'H is for Hawk' serves documented psychological resilience function in wildfire-prone communities
2017 wildfire response was most effective in communities with strong pre-existing social connections between informal leaders
discussion.casey_position: While informal resilience infrastructure is real and important, formal systems have significant limitations and gaps—passive monitoring without downstream social support, and volunteer-dependent networks lack sustainability or succession planning.
casey_key_evidence: Air quality advisories serve people with options (cars, sealed windows) but not vulnerable rural populations lacking resources to act on warnings
2017 wildfire Facebook admin who coordinated critical information was unfunded, unrecognized, and created succession planning fragility
Endowment and community-based funding models face barriers in smaller communities like Likely and Alexis Creek
discussion.resolution: Riley and Casey achieved consensus that Cariboo communities need both formal infrastructure (fire trucks, monitoring systems) and informal relational infrastructure simultaneously, and agreed that mapping and connecting existing informal leaders in non-crisis time is a high-leverage, low-cost intervention.
topics_covered: Fire response infrastructure and volunteer emergency services
Air quality monitoring and vulnerable population access to health information
Cultural institutions as psychological resilience resources
Community foundation endowment models for sustaining local infrastructure
Indigenous Guardian programs as models for sustained locally-rooted capacity
